text: We describe the case of a relaparoscopic approach of recurrence after laparoscopic incisional hernia repair. We would like to demonstrate the feasibility and benefits of the relaparoscopic approach of a recurrent incisional hernia originally treated by laparoscopy.
This is a case of a sixty-five-year-old male obese patient with history of multiple laparotomies who underwent laparoscopic repair of a paramedial hypogastric incisional hernia.
The surgeon performs dissection of adhesions and enterolysis, carefully separating the adhered tissues within the surgical field.
The surgeon performs primary closure of the abdominal wall defect following the resection of the hernia sac, preparing for the placement of a dual mesh.
After an eight-month follow-up period, a peri-umbilical recurrence was diagnosed and the patient re-operated by laparoscopy.
The surgeon dissects multiple adhesions to expose the hernia sac, preparing the surgical field for further repair.
The surgeon identifies a three-centimeter defect at the upper edge of the mesh and closes it using non-absorbable stitches, securing the mesh to the fascial margin.
A larger intraperitoneal dual mesh prosthesis is placed to cover the entire abdominal wall, following the primary closure of the wall defect.
No early either late complications were diagnosed and after one year of follow-up, the patient remains free of recurrence.

We can conclude saying that the use of a second intraperitoneal bigger mesh during a relaparoscopic approach of a recurrent incisional hernia already treated by laparoscopy appears to be safe and effective.
